name: WebSocketLive
tagline: Talk to a backend over a second protocol - a full-duplex WebSocket - streaming live messages instead of one request/response.
whatItDoes: The app opens on a single chat-style screen that immediately connects. A status line shows the live connection state - Disconnected, Connecting, Connected (green), or Failed (red). Below it a scrolling transcript fills the screen: messages you send are right-aligned and prefixed 'you:', messages from the server are left-aligned and prefixed 'server:'. At the bottom a text box plus a Send button let you type a message; the echo server sends the exact same text straight back, so each line you send appears twice - once as yours, once echoed. By default it runs offline against a fake echo client, so it connects and echoes with no network.
mentalModel: A WebSocket is a STREAM, not a single call: unlike HTTP request/response, one connection stays open and either side can push messages at any time - so model the connection as a Flow of events and fold that stream into one immutable UI state.

screens.name: LiveScreen
screens.role: Stateful entry composable: grabs the LiveViewModel, collects its uiState StateFlow, opens the connection once via LaunchedEffect, and owns the text-field input.

screens.name: LiveContent
screens.role: Stateless renderer: draws the status chip, the scrolling transcript (a LazyColumn), and the input row, reporting edits and sends through callbacks.

screens.name: MessageRow
screens.role: One transcript line - right-aligned and primary-coloured when it is from you, left-aligned and default when it came from the server.

screens.name: LiveContentConnectedPreview / LiveContentConnectingPreview
screens.role: Two @Preview functions that drive the stateless content with hand-made LiveUiState values, no real socket.

concepts.plain: HTTP (the NetworkParsing demo) is one request then one response. A WebSocket keeps a single connection open so the server can push messages whenever it likes - chat, live scores, presence. The screen models that as a sequence of events over time, not a single answer.

ConnectionEvent is a sealed interface listing everything the wire can report: Connecting, Open, MessageReceived(text), Closed(reason), Failure(error). The connection exposes them as a Flow<ConnectionEvent> the ViewModel collects.

RealWebSocketClient calls httpClient.newWebSocket(request, listener); the listener's onOpen / onMessage / onClosing / onFailure callbacks each tryEmit a ConnectionEvent into a MutableSharedFlow. tryEmit never suspends, so the OkHttp callback thread is never blocked.

reduceLiveState(state, event) is a plain function with no coroutines that folds one event into the next LiveUiState (status + transcript). Pulling the fold out as a pure function makes the whole state machine unit-testable without a socket or a dispatcher.

When you send, the ViewModel appends your line to the transcript immediately (fromMe = true) rather than waiting for the network. The server's echo arrives later as a MessageReceived and is appended by the reducer (fromMe = false).

LiveConnection is an interface with connect/send/close plus events(). RealWebSocketClient opens a live wss:// socket; FakeWebSocketClient scripts a connect and echoes sends with no network. provideLiveConnection(useFake) is the single switch - the default fake keeps the app and tests offline.

LiveScreen opens the socket with LaunchedEffect(Unit) { viewModel.connect() }. Keying on Unit means it runs exactly once when the screen first appears, not on every recomposition - the right place for a side effect like opening a connection.
collectAsStateWithLifecycle() reads the StateFlow but pauses while the screen is not visible, the safe way to observe a flow from Compose - the same pattern used across the other ViewModel demos.

files.role: The sealed ConnectionEvent (wire events) plus the ChatMessage and ConnectionStatus UI models the screen renders.
The LiveConnection interface plus RealWebSocketClient (OkHttp WebSocket), FakeWebSocketClient (offline echo), and provideLiveConnection(useFake) - the online/offline switch.
LiveUiState, the pure reduceLiveState fold, and the LiveViewModel that collects the event Flow into a StateFlow and exposes connect/send/disconnect.
The Compose UI: MainActivity, the stateful LiveScreen, the stateless LiveContent and MessageRow, plus @Preview functions.
Declares the INTERNET permission the real WebSocket needs and the MAIN/LAUNCHER intent-filter.
Project overview contrasting WebSocket with HTTP, and explaining the event-Flow, pure reducer, optimistic send, and Real-vs-Fake design.

highlights.title: Wire events modelled as a sealed stream type
highlights.explain: ConnectionEvent is the closed set of things a socket can report over time: Connecting and Open are data objects (no payload); MessageReceived, Closed and Failure carry a String. Because it is sealed, the reducer's 'when' over it is exhaustive - you cannot forget to handle one kind of event. This is the heart of treating a connection as a stream rather than a single call.

highlights.title: The LiveConnection contract: actions plus an events() Flow
highlights.explain: The ViewModel depends only on this interface: connect(), send(text), close() are the actions a screen can take, and events() returns a Flow<ConnectionEvent> it can collect. Hiding the protocol behind this tiny surface is what lets the real OkHttp socket and the offline fake be swapped with no change to the ViewModel or UI.

highlights.title: OkHttp callbacks funnelled into a Flow
highlights.explain: connect() builds a Request for the wss:// URL and calls newWebSocket with an anonymous WebSocketListener. Each callback - onOpen, onMessage, onClosing, onFailure - turns a raw socket event into a ConnectionEvent via events.tryEmit(...). tryEmit is used (not emit) because these callbacks run on OkHttp's own thread and must never suspend; the MutableSharedFlow's extra buffer capacity absorbs the events for the collector.

highlights.title: The offline fake echoes exactly like the real server
highlights.explain: FakeWebSocketClient implements the same interface with no network: connect() scripts Connecting -> Open -> a greeting, and send(text) emits the same text straight back as a MessageReceived - exactly what a real echo server does. Because it behaves identically to the real socket, the app runs fully offline and the unit tests can assert the connect/echo behaviour deterministically.

highlights.title: A pure reducer folds each event into one state
highlights.explain: reduceLiveState takes the current state plus one event and returns the next state - Open sets Connected, MessageReceived appends a 'not from me' line, Failure sets Failed, and so on. It has no coroutines and no side effects, so the entire state machine is trivially unit-testable; the ViewModel below does nothing more than apply this on every event.

highlights.title: Collecting the stream; optimistic send()
highlights.explain: In init the ViewModel launches one coroutine that collects connection.events() for its whole lifetime, folding each event into _uiState with reduceLiveState. send() guards against blank input, forwards the text to the connection, and OPTIMISTICALLY appends your own line (fromMe = true) right away; the server's echo comes back later through the same events() stream and is appended as a received line.

highlights.title: Open the socket once with LaunchedEffect
highlights.explain: LiveScreen collects the ViewModel's StateFlow with collectAsStateWithLifecycle(), then opens the connection exactly once via LaunchedEffect(Unit) { viewModel.connect() } - keyed on Unit so it does not re-fire on recomposition. It owns the input text in a remember { mutableStateOf("") } and clears it after each send, delegating all drawing to the stateless LiveContent.

newVsPrev: NetworkParsing spoke HTTP: one request, one parsed response, then done. WebSocketLive keeps the same Real-vs-Fake repository shape and the same ViewModel/StateFlow/collectAsStateWithLifecycle habits, but swaps the protocol for a full-duplex WebSocket (OkHttp WebSocket + WebSocketListener). The genuinely new ideas are: a connection modelled as a Flow of sealed ConnectionEvents instead of a single suspend call, a pure reduceLiveState that folds that stream into one immutable UiState, optimistic sending (your line appears before the echo returns), and LaunchedEffect to open the socket once. It is the same data-layer discipline applied to a streaming backend.
tryThis: In WebSocketClient.kt set provideLiveConnection's default to useFake = false, run on a device WITH internet, type a message, and watch the real wss://echo.websocket.events server echo it back over the open socket.
Still live, turn on airplane mode while connected and send a message: the listener's onFailure fires, the reducer flips the status chip to Failed (red), and the app stays alive instead of crashing.

In reduceLiveState change the MessageReceived branch to prefix received text (for example 'echo -> ' + event.text) and confirm only server lines change, proving the reducer is the single place inbound messages are shaped.
Send several messages quickly in fake mode and watch each one appear twice (your optimistic line, then the echo) - then remove the optimistic append in send() to feel why it is there.
Add a Disconnect button wired to viewModel.disconnect() and confirm the status chip returns to Disconnected via the Closed event.
gotchas: The app runs OFFLINE by default (useFake = true): the greeting and echoes come from FakeWebSocketClient, not a network. The fake behaves exactly like the real echo server, so you must flip the switch to actually open a wss:// socket.

events() is a hot MutableSharedFlow with no replay, so events emitted before a collector subscribes are dropped. The ViewModel works because its init collector subscribes first and connect() runs afterwards - the unit test reproduces this by subscribing (runCurrent) before emitting.

The real client targets wss://echo.websocket.events, a public third-party echo server - fine for a teaching demo, but do not send anything sensitive. The connection is encrypted (wss = WebSocket over TLS), so no cleartext-traffic config is needed.

tryEmit (not emit) is used inside the OkHttp listener because those callbacks run on a non-suspending socket thread; the SharedFlow's extraBufferCapacity is what lets tryEmit succeed without dropping events.
runTest: Build and run from the project root with ./gradlew installDebug (or ./gradlew assembleDebug to just compile). Launch the app: by default it runs offline, so the status should move to Connected and a 'Connected to the OFFLINE fake echo server.' line appears; type a message and tap Send to see it echoed straight back. To exercise the live path, set provideLiveConnection(useFake = false) in WebSocketClient.kt and run on a networked device against wss://echo.websocket.events. Run the unit tests (reducer, fake stream, ViewModel) with ./gradlew testDebugUnitTest - they need no internet.
